text: The surgeon performs an intrafacial nerve-sparing cystectomy, starting with a medial incision in the posterior rectovesicle peritoneum to develop a medial space. The seminal vesicles and vasa are mobilized, and the pedicle to the seminal vesicles is controlled using either haemlock or metallic clips. Retraction is applied to the seminal vesicles, and the fold of Denonvilliers fascia is incised at the apex. The intrafascial plane is identified and dissected distally and laterally, with vessels entering the base of the prostate medially being controlled as needed.

The surgeon mobilizes the seminal vesicles and vasa, developing a medial space. The prostatic capsule is sharply incised above the fold of Denonvilliers fascia to enter a plane between the central and peripheral zones of the prostate. The peripheral zone is identified by its yellow appearance. The dissection is carried distally and laterally using a combination of blunt and sharp techniques, with occasional use of a thirty-degree lens for improved visualization. The plane is developed towards the apex and laterally as far as possible, completing the medial space development before proceeding with the cystectomy.

The surgeon performs prostate capsule sparing with sparing of the seminal vesicles. A medial space is developed above the seminal vesicles and vasa. The prostate capsule is incised at the entry point of these structures into the prostate, forming the ejaculatory duct. The peripheral zone, identified by its yellow appearance, is separated from the central and transitional zones using blunt and sharp dissection. The ejaculatory ducts are visible in the midline and are divided, followed by the division of the vasa, which serves as a landmark for further dissection.

The surgeon develops lateral bladder spaces after creating a medial space, dividing the vasa and dissecting towards the endopelvic fascia. Peritoneal incisions are joined, leaving a strip over the ureter for anastomosis. The ureter is clipped and divided, and bladder pedicles are divided using haemlock clips and a robotic vessel sealer. Dissection is performed above the seminal vesicles and divided vas to protect neurovascular bundles, continuing until reaching the prostate base. The bladder is released from anterior attachments with medial retraction of the prostate and division of vascular structures high on the prostate, completing the process on the contralateral side.

The surgeon defats the anterior prostate to expose the intact puboprostatic ligaments. A sub-DVC dissection is performed to limit bleeding, followed by apical dissection until reaching the urethra. The catheter is clipped and cut to prevent urine spillage, and remaining attachments are divided. The specimen is placed in a retrieval bag, and the DVC complex is over-sewn. The visual appearance reflects sparing of the prostate capsule and seminal vesicles, as well as intrafascial nerve sparing.
